front: Work: Julien Sorel is executed after shooting Madame de Renal in this novel
Work: Mathilde de la Mole kisses the severed head of her executed lover and buries it in a marble cave, imitating the legend of her ancestor and Queen Margot
Work: Mathilde de la Mole becomes engaged to Monsieur de Croisenois despite loving the protagonist, who courts Madame de Fervaques to make her jealous
Work: a carpenter's son becomes tutor to the mayor's children, loses his job when M. Valenod discovers his affair with the mayor's wife
Work: the title colors of this novel symbolize the French army (red) and the Catholic church (black)
Work: the seminary director Monsieur Pirard resigns in disgust at church politics in this novel
Work: the relationship between the protagonist and Mathilde begins when they catch each other stealing volumes of Voltaire from a library
Work: this novel was placed on the Catholic Church's Index of banned books more than a century after publication
Work: the epigraph to the American Gigolo screenplay is a quote about this novel's protagonist, and the film's protagonist is named after him
back: The Red and the Black (Stendhal) [character: Julien Sorel]

front: Work: Fabrice del Dongo puts on a dead hussar's uniform and stumbles into the Battle of Waterloo
Work: the protagonist is imprisoned in a tower and falls in love with the bird-tending Clelia, communicating by holding up large lettered signs
Work: features the Countess Gina and her lover Count Mosca, who help the protagonist
Work: Giletti, protector of the actress Marietta Valsera, is killed in a scuffle with the protagonist
Work: the protagonist joins Napoleon's Hundred Days campaign in this novel, and ultimately becomes a monk
Work: this treatise describes 'crystallization' as a metaphor for falling in love, inspired by tree branches left in a Salzburg salt mine
Work: in this treatise, the author sketched a diagram on a playing card depicting crystallization as a journey from Bologna to Rome
Work: the stages of crystallization in this treatise include Admiration, Acknowledgement, Hope, and Delight
Work: this treatise illustrates the idea that beauty is 'the promise of happiness' with an anecdote about Alberic admiring his mistress's smallpox scar
Author: this author's namesake 'syndrome' describes the phenomenon of being overwhelmed by great art, inspired by his near-fainting in Florence's Basilica of Santa Croce
Author: W.G. Sebald's novel Vertigo opens with a biography of this author, including a photograph of the plaster cast of Metilde Dembowski's hand from his desk
Author: this author's autobiography The Life of Henry Brulard was disguised as 'a novel imitating The Vicar of Wakefield'
Author: this author discussed his feelings for Metilde Dembowski in the essay 'Maxims of Love'
Author: this author described seducing his lovers by reading to them from Rousseau's The New Heloise
Author: this author's first novel Armance follows Octave de Malivert, who falls in love with the title character before leaving to fight for Greek independence
Author: this author's unfinished novel Lamiel follows an orphan who leaves Normandy to find 'what love is all about'
Author: the Cenci murder inspired an 1837 short story by this author
Author: this author also wrote the novella Vanina Vanini and the unfinished The Pink and the Green
